nativeRange.habitat: Generally found in rocky areas of seaward reefs and coral-rich slopes at depths of approximately 3-30 m. Often observed grazing in the open near patches of live rock.

diet.notes: Has been documented grazing primarily on microalgae and detritus from live rock, supplemented by small crustaceans and zooplankton. In captivity, generally advised to offer a varied diet including Spirulina-based preparations, marine algae (nori), mysis shrimp, and other meaty frozen foods. Frequent small feedings (2-3 times daily) are commonly recommended to support the grazing lifestyle.

summary: A dwarf angelfish from the eastern Indian Ocean with a pale silver-grey body marked by orange-red flank stripes and a dark caudal blotch. Generally considered intermediate in difficulty; best kept singly in a well-established reef tank with abundant live rock for grazing.
careNotes:
The Red Stripe Angelfish (also widely sold as Eibli Angelfish or Eibl's Angelfish, and sometimes as Blacktail Angelfish in reference to its dark tail patch) is generally considered a moderately hardy dwarf angel. It tends to be docile when first introduced but can become assertive toward smaller or similarly coloured tankmates once established. As with most Centropyge, it is best kept as the only dwarf angelfish in the system; conspecifics and other similarly patterned dwarf angels are generally advised to be avoided unless the tank is very large.

In the wild it has been reported to form harems of 3-7 individuals (one male with multiple females), but harem-keeping in home aquaria typically requires very large systems and is uncommon. Like other Centropyge it is a protogynous hermaphrodite, with dominant individuals transitioning from female to male. Notable natural hybridization with Centropyge vrolikii (Half-Black Angelfish) and C. flavissima has been documented where ranges overlap.

Regarding reef safety, this species is commonly considered reef safe with caution. Isolated reports of nipping at clam mantles, soft corals, and large-polyp stony corals are well documented, though some specimens reportedly coexist with corals without incident. A well-matured aquarium with plentiful live rock for grazing and a varied, algae-rich diet may reduce the likelihood of nipping behavior.

Cross-source data notes: LiveAquaria states adult size up to ~15 cm (6 in), 70 US gal (~265 L) minimum tank, 22-26°C; FishBase reports max length 15 cm TL and wild temperature 25.2-29.3°C; ReefApp states 16 cm max length, 300 L minimum tank, and approximately 26°C. The averaged temperature range used here is approximately 24-27°C, and the conservative minimum tank size of 300 L is used given the species' size, activity, and need for grazing space.
breedingNotes: Generally reported to be extremely difficult to breed in home aquaria, with no reliable external sexual dimorphism (males in dominant phase may be slightly larger and more colourful). Like other dwarf angelfishes, it is a protogynous hermaphrodite. Successful captive spawning reports are rare and typically involve very large, mature systems.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name (Centropyge eibli Klausewitz, 1963), taxonomy (Pomacanthidae / Acanthuriformes), native range (Eastern Indian Ocean: Sri Lanka to Western Australia, including Indonesia and Andaman Islands), max length 15 cm TL, wild temperature 25.2-29.3°C, and harem behavior (groups of 3-7).

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Substitute secondary after designated Reef2Reef wiki URL returned 403. Confirmed adult size (16 cm), reef-safe status (with caution / 'reef safe with luck'), minimum tank volume 300 L, salinity SG ~1.026, temperature ~26°C. The 300 L tank minimum was preferred over LiveAquaria's 265 L per the playbook's tank-size bias-larger rule.

fish.breedingNotes: Has been reported to be rarely bred in captivity; no reliable external sexual dimorphism. Protogynous hermaphrodite in the wild. Successful captive spawning attempts are uncommon and usually require very large mature systems.
